This one is challenging. It matches all the inclusion criteria, but compares the variance of intervention effects instead of the mean effects. This means it does not fit the research purpose. Since it still perfectly matches the criteria, I decide to include it, but I strongly suggest to exclude it later during data extraction. Maybe we need to create a new exclusion category of articles excluded during extraction due to reasons not covered by the screening.

Okay, this article has three MAs: 1+2 are cross-sectional, they do have only one time point of measurement. MA 3 deals with intervention studies, but only aggregates within group changes for intervention and control groups, respectively, without statistically comparing them. So the MA either has no pre-post design, or has no group comparison.

- Edit: Okay, I realized that only because this MA does not use a statistical model to compare the intervention with the control group does not mean that it should be excluded. In other words, the design matches the designs we are looking for, and that it was not analyzed in a way we are interested in is a problem for data extraction rather than screening. So this may be an article to exclude during extraction.
As far as I can see most studies have no control group, and thus no group comparisons exist. Only within group comparisons (e.g., changes in the intervention group).

Difficult on different levels. For me the most decisive aspect is that there are no exercise intervention. The interventions are anti-doping programs and the outcome is doping intention. This is of cause related to exercise, but for me not enough to be considered an "exercise intervention". More a behavioral intervention to impact the susceptibility to cheating.
most of the MAs in this article focus on within-group changes, but some also compare intervention groups

Two remarks here: The intervention is a a adjust treatments+exercise, but this is enough for me to be considered an exercise intervention (or a variant hereof). Also, it is not explicitly stated that the baseline values are used for the analysis, but since only RCTs are included and the authors state "Data were extracted for all evaluated timepoints (...)" I consider this to deal with pre-post designs.

Difficult... The first MA in the article only investigates within group changes, so there is no group comparison. The second MA (Fig 4) matches the pre-post group comparison design (though it seems to be cross-over, which still means inclusion. BUT: Fig4 includes multiple comparisons from each study within the analysis with reverted signs, i.e., for one effect size one group is defined as control and the other as intervention, and for the next effect size the group definitions are reverted. This has no direct impact on our research question but certainly invalidates this particular MA.
